facts_disclosed: BSc Physio at UWE, third year, wants community physio, 60 quid/session reference

turns.content: yo so i got a quick question about something if thats cool. im trying to figure out how to explain what i want to do career wise because people keep asking and i feel like i ramble you know
Of course, happy to help you work on that. What's your field, and what do you want to do after you finish studying?

yeah so im doing physiotherapy at UWE. third year now init so its getting proper serious with placements and all that. like i love it dont get me wrong the placements are intense but thats where you actually learn tbh
Nice, third year physio is a big step -- that's when it all starts coming together with the clinical experience. So when people ask what you want to do after graduating, what do you usually say?

thats the thing right i start going off on one about community physio and how i dont want private practice and people just look at me blank. like most people hear physio and think oh you want to work with footballers or have a nice clinic in clifton charging bare money
I get that. There's definitely a perception that physio is either elite sport or expensive private clinics. So you're more interested in community-based work -- what draws you to that specifically?

because thats where people actually need it bruv. like if youre a premiership footballer youve got physios dietitians sports scientists the whole package yeah. but the normal person who does their back in at work or the kid who gets injured playing sunday league they cant just rock up to a private clinic and pay 60 quid a session can they. thats not accessible thats just healthcare for people with money

That's a really clear and compelling way to put it. You're highlighting the gap between who has access to quality rehab and who doesn't. That's actually a strong pitch -- you could frame it as wanting to bring specialist-level physio into community settings where people can't afford private rates. Have you thought about where you'd want to do that kind of work?

bristol man. this is my city you know. theres bare communities here that are underserved when it comes to physio and rehab. like the NHS waiting lists are a joke you could be waiting months for a physio appointment and if you cant afford private youre just stuck. i want to be in those communities making it happen

That's a strong vision and it sounds like you already know your audience and your why. For your elevator pitch, you could keep it tight: you're training as a physio because you want to make quality rehabilitation accessible to communities that are currently priced out of it. You don't need to explain what you don't want -- lead with what you do want and why it matters.

yeah thats actually bare helpful tbh because i always start by saying what i dont want which probably sounds negative init. lead with what i do want. okay yeah i can work with that. its just hard because i get passionate about it and then i go off track you know

The passion is a good thing, don't lose that. People respond to someone who genuinely cares about what they're doing. Just channel it -- start with the vision, then back it up with your experience from placements. The passion fills in the gaps naturally. You don't need to convince anyone that the system is broken, just show them you're the person who's going to do something about it.

icl thats proper motivating. yeah alright im going to practice that. start with the vision not the rant haha. cheers for this man appreciate it. got a placement session tomorrow morning so i should probably get some sleep anyway
Good luck on placement tomorrow. You've clearly got the drive and the clarity about what you want to do -- just need to package it up. Go get some rest and smash it in the morning.
